name: Harvard University
notes: Harvard University was founded in 1636 and is the oldest institution of higher learning in the United States. The university is devoted to excellence in teaching, learning, and research, and to developing leaders in many disciplines who make a difference globally. Harvard employs 12,899 people and has an annual revenue of $6.1B. The university operates in 134 countries including United Kingdom, India, Canada, Brazil, and China.

name: Massachusetts Institute of Technology
notes: The Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) is a private research university in Cambridge, Massachusetts, United States, founded in 1861. MIT's mission is to advance knowledge and educate students in science, technology, and other areas of scholarship that will best serve the nation and the world in the 21st century. The Institute has 11,886 total students as of 2024-2025, including 4,535 undergraduates and 7,351 graduate students. MIT employs 11,147 people and has an annual revenue of $3.9B.

name: Princeton University
notes: Princeton University is chartered in 1746, making it the fourth-oldest college in the United States. The University advances learning through scholarship, research, and teaching of unsurpassed quality, with an emphasis on undergraduate and doctoral education. Princeton employs 7,123 people and has an annual revenue of $3.6B. The Princeton Laboratory for Artificial Intelligence supports and expands the scope of AI research at Princeton, adopting a new organizational model designed to accommodate the challenges of working in this fast-moving field.

name: Stanford University
notes: Stanford University was founded in 1885 by Leland and Jane Stanford as a tribute to the memory of their only child, Leland Stanford Jr. The university officially opened in 1891 on the Stanfords' former Palo Alto farm. Stanford is organized around seven schools of study on an 8,180-acre campus, one of the largest in the nation. The university houses multiple AI-focused institutions including the Stanford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (SAIL), founded in 1962, and the Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence (HAI), founded in 2019.

name: UC Berkeley
notes: UC Berkeley is a public research university founded in 1868 and headquartered in Berkeley, California. The university employs 13,995 people with an annual revenue of $2.4B and operates in 83 countries. UC Berkeley is a leading institution in artificial intelligence research, hosting multiple AI-focused research centers including the Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research (BAIR) Lab, which brings together over 50 faculty and 300+ graduate students across areas like computer vision, machine learning, natural language processing, planning, control, and robotics. The university has nearly 50 graduate programs ranked in the top 10 by U.S. News 2025 rankings, including Computer Science (#2), Engineering (#3), and Public Affairs (#3).

name: University of Cambridge
notes: The University of Cambridge is one of the world's oldest universities and leading academic centres, established in 1209. The University comprises 31 autonomous Colleges and over 100 departments, faculties and institutions. Its 25,000 students include around 11,000 international students. The University has an annual income of £2 billion.

name: University of Oxford
notes: The University of Oxford is described as "the oldest university in the English-speaking world" and "a world-leading centre of learning, teaching and research". Oxford has announced plans to expand its artificial intelligence capabilities through a five-year collaboration with OpenAI, providing students and faculty access to research grant funding, enterprise-level security and cutting-edge AI tools. The university is ranked number one in the world in the 2025 Times Higher Education World Rankings and is at the forefront of academic disciplines including medical sciences, mathematical and physical sciences, humanities, and social sciences. Oxford admitted 3,245 students in 2024, with approximately 80.5% of undergraduate places going to UK students.

name: Center for AI Safety (CAIS)
notes: The Center for AI Safety (CAIS) is an American nonprofit organization based in San Francisco that promotes the safe development and deployment of artificial intelligence. It was founded in 2022 by Dan Hendrycks and Oliver Zhang. CAIS' mission is to reduce societal-scale risks associated with AI by conducting safety research, building the field of AI safety researchers, and advocating for safety standards. In May 2023, CAIS published the statement on AI risk of extinction signed by hundreds of professors of AI, leaders of major AI companies, and other public figures.

name: Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI)
notes: Founded 2000 by Eliezer Yudkowsky, 24-year history, ~20 staff. Major shift in 2023-2024 from technical research to communications and policy work. $16M reserves, historically funded by Open Philanthropy ($15.9M), Vitalik Buterin ($5.4M), Jaan Tallinn ($1.6M). Published influential works including 'AGI Ruin: A List of Lethalities.' Launched Technical Governance Team in 2024 focusing on international AI agreements and verification mechanisms. CEO Malo Bourgon took leadership in 2024.

name: Alphabet Inc.
notes: Parent company of Google, Google DeepMind, and other subsidiaries. One of the world's largest AI developers and deployers. Published AI Principles in 2018; controversially removed pledges against AI use in weapons and surveillance in February 2025, drawing criticism from lawmakers and civil society. Supports 'multi-stakeholder' AI governance: risk assessments for high-risk systems, sector-specific regulation, but lobbied for 10-year ban on state-level AI regulation in U.S. House package. Publishes annual Responsible AI Progress Reports. Google DeepMind leadership (Hassabis, Manyika) stated democracies should lead AI development guided by freedom, equality, and human rights.

name: Amazon
notes: Amazon has developed frontier AI models through its Nova family and operates Amazon Bedrock AI service. Published comprehensive Frontier Model Safety Framework in 2024 with critical capability thresholds for CBRN weapons, cybersecurity, and automated AI R&D. AWS CEO Andy Jassy projects AI could help AWS reach $600 billion in annual sales by 2036, double previous estimates. Major cloud infrastructure provider enabling AI development across the industry.

name: Apple
notes: Consumer technology company deploying AI via Apple Intelligence across its device ecosystem. Pursues an on-device, privacy-first AI strategy using Private Cloud Compute for sensitive tasks. Partnered with OpenAI (ChatGPT integration) and Google (Gemini for Siri). Avoids public regulatory advocacy but leverages privacy standards as a competitive differentiator.

name: Palantir
notes: Palantir Technologies is a data analytics and AI company that builds software platforms for data integration and analysis, serving government and commercial clients. The company was founded in 2003 by Peter Thiel and others, with Alex Karp joining as CEO in 2004. Palantir's Maven AI system has been adopted by the Pentagon as a core military system for weapons targeting and battlefield data analysis. The company reported Q4 2025 revenue of $1.41 billion, up 70% year-over-year, with a market capitalization of approximately $100.6 billion.

name: Tesla
notes: Tesla announced a joint project with xAI called Digital Optimus, also nicknamed 'Macrohard,' designed to automate complex office workflows by observing and replicating human interactions with computers. As part of this collaboration, Tesla made a $2 billion investment in xAI in 2026. Tesla's financial performance declined significantly in 2025, with profits falling 46 percent and revenue dropping for the first time in the company's history. The company's automotive revenues fell by 11 percent to $17.7 billion in Q4 2025, though energy storage business grew 25 percent.

name: ACLU
notes: The American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) is a nonpartisan nonprofit and 501(c)(4) advocacy organization defending constitutional rights. Its Speech, Privacy & Technology Project focuses on AI-driven surveillance, opposing facial recognition use by law enforcement, and calling for targeted civil rights protections. The ACLU supports the AI Civil Rights Act of 2025, partners with the AI Now Institute, and is funded through membership dues, individual donations, and private foundations.

name: Electronic Frontier Foundation
notes: Leading digital rights nonprofit founded in 1990. Opposes AI-powered surveillance and use of AI in high-stakes government decisions. Advocates for civil-liberties-based, targeted AI regulation protecting privacy and due process.

name: Anthropic
notes: Anthropic is an artificial intelligence company founded in 2021 by former OpenAI leaders including Dario Amodei and Daniela Amodei. The company specializes in Constitutional AI and has developed Claude, a family of large language models designed for safety and enterprise use. Anthropic has experienced rapid growth, scaling from $1 billion ARR in 2024 to $14 billion in February 2026. The company is structured as a Public Benefit Corporation focused on AI safety research.

name: DeepSeek
notes: DeepSeek was founded in 2023 in Hangzhou, China by Liang Wenfeng, who previously co-founded one of China's top hedge funds, High-Flyer, which focuses on AI-driven quantitative trading. The company released its first AI large language model later in 2023. DeepSeek operates independently but is financially backed by High-Flyer, receiving a significant $50 million investment from the hedge fund with no institutional or angel investors. The company is fully funded by High-Flyer, which manages approximately Rmb60 billion (around $8 billion) as of 2023.

name: Google
notes: Google is an American multinational technology corporation focused on artificial intelligence and other technologies. The company was founded in 1998 by Larry Page and Sergey Brin. Sundar Pichai was appointed CEO of Google in 2015, replacing Larry Page, and in 2019, Pichai also became the CEO of Alphabet. Google is Alphabet's largest subsidiary and is a holding company for Alphabet's internet properties and interests.

name: Google DeepMind
notes: Google DeepMind brings together two of the world's leading AI labs — Google Brain and DeepMind — into a single, focused team led by CEO Demis Hassabis. The company's mission is to build AI responsibly to benefit humanity, working to advance artificial general intelligence (AGI). Google DeepMind has achieved notable breakthroughs including AlphaGo defeating world champion Go players, AlphaFold solving protein folding problems, and developing the Transformer architecture that underlies most large language models. The organization employs over 1,000 researchers and engineers from diverse disciplines and maintains partnerships with academic institutions, healthcare organizations, and government agencies.

name: Meta
notes: Meta Platforms, Inc. is the developer of the Meta AI app and various AI products including Meta AI assistant built with Llama technology. The company develops and releases the Llama series of open-source large language models, with Llama 3 and Llama 4 being their latest offerings. Meta's mission is to build the future of human connection and the technology that makes it possible. The company reported revenue of $200.97 billion for full year 2025, representing a 22% year-over-year increase.

name: Meta AI
notes: Meta AI is a research division of Meta (formerly Facebook) that develops artificial intelligence and augmented reality technologies. The division was founded on December 11, 2015, with founders including Rob Fergus, Yann LeCun, Serkan Piantino, and Mark Zuckerberg. Meta AI has workspaces in Menlo Park, London, New York City, Paris, Seattle, Pittsburgh, Tel Aviv, and Montreal as of 2025. The organization produces products including Llama and operates under the ownership of Meta Platforms.

name: Microsoft
notes: Microsoft Corporation is an American multinational technology conglomerate headquartered in Redmond, Washington. Founded in 1975, the company became influential in the rise of personal computers through software like Windows, and has since expanded to Internet services, cloud computing, artificial intelligence, video gaming, and other fields. Microsoft is the largest software company by revenue, one of the most valuable public companies, and one of the most valuable brands globally. Revenue was $281.7 billion, up 15 percent in fiscal 2025.

name: OpenAI
notes: OpenAI is an American artificial intelligence research organization comprising both a nonprofit foundation and a controlled for-profit public benefit corporation, headquartered in San Francisco. The company was founded in December 2015 as a nonprofit, with a for-profit subsidiary created in 2019, and restructured in 2025 to operate more independently from the nonprofit. OpenAI's mission is to ensure that artificial general intelligence benefits all of humanity. The company has 4,500 employees as of 2026 and reported revenue of US$13.1 billion in 2025.

name: xAI
notes: xAI is an American AI company founded by Elon Musk on March 9, 2023, with a mission to "understand the true nature of the universe". SpaceX acquired xAI in February 2026 in an all-stock transaction that valued SpaceX at $1 trillion and xAI at $250 billion. The company's flagship product is Grok, an AI chatbot that is accessible on grok.com, iOS, and Android. xAI raised $20 billion in a Series E funding round in January 2026, with strategic investors including Nvidia and Cisco Investments.

name: Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI)
notes: The Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) is a body within the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) at the U.S. Department of Commerce. It was established on June 3, 2025 when Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick renamed and refocused the U.S. AI Safety Institute (AISI), which had been created under the Biden administration in November 2023 by Commerce Secretary Gina Raimondo. The rebrand dropped "safety" from the title and pivoted the mission from catastrophic-risk research toward national security, cybersecurity, chemical/biological weapons misuse evaluation, and global competitiveness with China. CAISI serves as industry's primary U.S. government point of contact for voluntary testing and collaborative research on commercial AI systems, develops security guidelines, conducts capability and vulnerability evaluations, and represents U.S. interests in international AI standards bodies. Inaugural AISI director Elizabeth Kelly — who helped author the 2023 Biden AI executive order — led the institute from February 2024 until her departure on February 5, 2025 following the Trump transition; no public successor director has been confirmed as of early 2026. Under Kelly, AISI reached pre-deployment testing agreements with OpenAI and Anthropic and launched the Testing Risks of AI for National Security (TRAINS) Taskforce in November 2024. Paul Christiano and other AISI-era staff were hired prior to the rebrand.

name: Department of Commerce
notes: Houses NIST, which develops the AI Risk Management Framework, and BIS, which controls AI chip exports. Under Trump, overseeing the American AI Exports Program and revising NIST's AI RMF to remove DEI and climate references.

name: Department of Defense
notes: The Department of Defense is the largest U.S. federal agency and a major driver of AI policy and procurement. In January 2026, Secretary Hegseth released an "AI-first" strategy directing the department to adopt AI at wartime speed, including deploying frontier models to warfighters within 30 days of public release. The Chief Digital and AI Office (CDAO) was realigned under the Under Secretary for R&E/CTO Emil Michael with a Barrier Removal Board to accelerate AI delivery. DoD AI work spans autonomous systems, logistics, intelligence, and enterprise operations across seven pace-setting projects.

name: Department of Justice
notes: Federal law enforcement agency operating the AI Litigation Task Force (est. Jan 2026) to challenge state AI laws. Pursues AI antitrust cases and False Claims Act enforcement; historically oversaw civil rights compliance with law enforcement AI use.

name: European AI Office
notes: Established February 2024 within European Commission as world's first comprehensive AI regulatory body. Employs 125+ staff including technology specialists, lawyers, and economists across 6 units. Enforces EU AI Act (entered force August 2024) with phased implementation through 2027. Led by Director Lucilla Sioli, coordinates with European AI Board representing 27 member states.

name: European Commission
notes: EU executive body responsible for implementing the AI Act, which entered into force August 2024. Key 2025 milestones: prohibited practices applied February 2025, GPAI model obligations August 2025. Full application by August 2026. Issues binding guidelines and manages the AI Office for enforcement. Proposed Digital Omnibus amendments in November 2025 to adjust high-risk system timelines.

name: European Union
notes: The European Union is a political and economic union of 27 member states that are located primarily in Europe. The EU was established, along with its citizenship, when the Maastricht Treaty came into force in 1993, and was incorporated as an international legal juridical person upon entry into force of the Treaty of Lisbon in 2009. In 2012, the EU was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize. The European Union has enacted the Artificial Intelligence Act, which is a European Union regulation concerning artificial intelligence that entered into force on 1 August 2024.

name: Federal Trade Commission
notes: The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) is an independent U.S. federal agency responsible for consumer protection and preventing anticompetitive business practices. In the AI policy ecosystem, the FTC is a primary enforcement body for AI-related consumer harms, using Section 5 of the FTC Act — which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices — as its principal enforcement vehicle. Under Biden-era Chair Lina Khan (2021–2025), the FTC took an aggressive posture on AI and Big Tech. Under Trump-appointed Chair Andrew Ferguson (2025–present), the FTC shifted to a lighter-touch model: enforcing existing consumer protection law against AI fraud and deception without pursuing new AI-specific rulemaking. In March 2026, the FTC issued a Policy Statement clarifying how Section 5 applies to AI systems. The agency's AI focus areas include algorithmic discrimination, deceptive AI-generated content, and privacy violations in AI data collection.

name: National Institute of Standards and Technology
notes: NIST develops voluntary AI standards and risk management frameworks for federal agencies and industry. Its AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) is widely adopted. In 2025-26 its AI Safety Institute was renamed the Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) under the Trump administration's AI Action Plan, with a mandate to revise the AI RMF.

name: Trump administration
notes: The Trump administration (second term, 2025-) has pursued an aggressive pro-innovation, deregulatory AI agenda. Day 1: revoked Biden's AI EO 14110 (EO 14148, Jan 20 2025). EO 14179 (Jan 23 2025) 'Removing Barriers to American Leadership in AI' launched an action plan for global AI dominance. OSTP released 90-point AI Action Plan (July 2025) with three pillars: Accelerating Innovation, Building AI Infrastructure, Leading International Diplomacy/Security. Dec 2025 EO 'Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI' attempted to preempt state AI regulation, threatening to withhold federal funds from states with 'conflicting' AI laws. Drew pushback from Republican governors (DeSantis, Cox). Cross-cutting priorities: protecting workers, ensuring trustworthy/unbiased AI, safeguarding against misuse. DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) pursuing separate AI-for-government-reform agenda.

name: UK AI Security Institute
notes: The UK AI Security Institute (AISI) is a government research organisation within the UK's Department for Science, Innovation and Technology, established in 2023 as the AI Safety Institute and renamed in February 2025. AISI's mission is to equip governments with scientific understanding of advanced AI risks — particularly catastrophic risks such as AI-assisted development of chemical and biological weapons, cyberattacks, and AI-enabled crime. Under Chair Ian Hogarth and Interim Director Adam Beaumont (formerly GCHQ's Chief AI Officer), the institute employs 100+ technical staff including alumni from OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Oxford. Key programs include the £15m Alignment Project, £8m Systemic Safety Grants, and £5m Challenge Fund. AISI holds formal research MOUs with Anthropic, Google DeepMind, OpenAI, and Cohere, and co-led the International AI Safety Report (2024). It plays a central role in the international AI Safety Summit process and directly informs UK government AI policy.

name: United Nations
notes: The United Nations is an international organization founded in 1945, currently made up of 193 Member States, with work guided by the purposes and principles contained in its founding Charter. The UN has established a High-Level Advisory Body on AI to analyze the current situation and recommend governance approaches for artificial intelligence. The General Assembly approved a $3.72 billion budget for the United Nations for 2025. The UN Charter was signed on 26 June 1945 in San Francisco and came into force on 24 October, which is now recognized as UN Day.

name: United States Senate
notes: The United States Senate is a legislative chamber in the bicameral legislature of the United States of America, and together with the U.S. House of Representatives makes up the U.S. Congress. First convened in 1789, the composition and powers of the Senate are established in Article One of the U.S. Constitution. Each U.S. state is represented by two senators, regardless of population, who serve staggered six-year terms. The chamber of the United States Senate is located in the north wing of the Capitol, in Washington, D.C., the national capital.

name: U.S. House of Representatives
notes: The U.S. House of Representatives passed a federal budget bill that includes a controversial 10-year moratorium on states regulating artificial intelligence with a vote of 215 to 214. The House established a Bipartisan Task Force on Artificial Intelligence that delivered a comprehensive report in December 2024 with 66 findings and 89 recommendations. The House held hearings on AI topics including "Advancing America's AI Action Plan" and "Building an AI-Ready America" in January 2026. The 119th Congress was sworn-in in January 2025, with Republicans holding a majority with 220 seats and Democrats holding 215 seats.

name: White House
notes: The White House released its National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence on March 20, 2026, outlining the Trump administration's legislative priorities for federal AI governance. The framework stems from an executive order President Donald Trump signed in December that blocked states from enforcing their own regulations around artificial intelligence. The White House established the Genesis Mission in November 2025 as a national effort to accelerate the application of AI for transformative scientific discovery focused on pressing national challenges. The administration has implemented America's AI Action Plan, which recognizes the need to invest in AI-enabled science to accelerate scientific advancement.

name: White House Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP)
notes: Led by Director Michael Kratsios under Trump administration. Coordinates AI policy across federal agencies through America's AI Action Plan (July 2025). Key initiatives include removing regulatory barriers, establishing AI procurement standards, creating federal AI framework to preempt state laws, and promoting AI adoption in government. Staff of approximately 22 people with $325M proposed budget cuts to NIST creating tension with innovation goals.

name: ASML
notes: Dutch semiconductor equipment manufacturer holding a global monopoly on Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV) lithography machines, essential for manufacturing advanced AI chips at nodes below 7nm. Founded 1984, headquartered in Veldhoven, Netherlands. EUV machines cost up to $400M each. Central to U.S.-China chip export control dynamics — the Dutch government has blocked EUV exports to China since 2019 under U.S. pressure. Current CEO Christophe Fouquet (since 2024) and predecessor Peter Wennink have both opposed further export restrictions, arguing they are economically motivated and could push China to develop its own chipmaking capabilities. ASML's monopoly position makes it a potential EU leverage point for AI safety and compute governance policy. NOTE: Miscategorized as VC/Capital/Philanthropy — should be Infrastructure & Compute.

name: Nvidia
notes: Founded 1993, became dominant AI chip provider with 92% discrete GPU market share in 2025. Market cap reached $4.6 trillion. Invested in 67+ AI startups in 2025, including OpenAI ($100M), Anthropic ($10B), and xAI ($6B). CEO Jensen Huang lobbied Trump administration to approve H200 chip sales to China, facing bipartisan congressional opposition over national security concerns.

name: TSMC
notes: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) is the world's largest contract chip manufacturer, fabricating over 90% of advanced semiconductors as of 2023. Founded 1987, manufactures chips for Nvidia, Apple, AMD and other AI companies. Revenue $122.4B in 2025, planning $56B capex in 2026. Critical supplier for AI infrastructure but not an AI research lab.

name: AFL-CIO
notes: Nation's largest labor federation representing 63 unions and nearly 15 million workers. Launched 'Workers First Initiative on AI' in October 2025 with comprehensive principles for worker-centered AI. Created AFL-CIO Technology Institute in 2021 and AI task force in 2024 to fight for state-level AI safety laws.

name: SAG-AFTRA
notes: SAG-AFTRA represents approximately 160,000 actors, announcers, broadcast journalists, dancers, DJs, news writers, news editors, program hosts, puppeteers, recording artists, singers, stunt performers, voiceover artists and other entertainment and media professionals. SAG-AFTRA was formed in 2012 through the merger of Screen Actors Guild (founded 1933) and American Federation of Television and Radio Artists (founded 1937). The union is a proud affiliate of the AFL-CIO and has national offices in Los Angeles and New York with local offices nationwide. SAG-AFTRA has been actively negotiating AI protections for performers, including updated rules in 2026 that require informed consent for digital replicas and appropriate compensation.

name: Bloomberg
notes: Bloomberg has extensive AI coverage across multiple verticals including technology, markets, and legal analysis. Operates dedicated AI section with regular reporting on policy developments, market trends, and corporate adoption. Bloomberg Law provides specialized AI legal analysis. The company has also developed its own AI products including ASKB for the Bloomberg Terminal, built on proprietary AI capabilities dating back to 2009.

name: The New York Times
notes: The New York Times is a media organization dedicated to seeking the truth and helping people understand the world through independent journalism. The company has over 11.4 million subscribers and employs approximately 2,000 journalists in its newsroom. The New York Times has created an A.I. Initiatives team led by Editorial Director Zach Seward to leverage artificial intelligence for journalism while maintaining human oversight. The organization filed a lawsuit against Perplexity AI for unauthorized use of Times content.

name: Wall Street Journal
notes: Major U.S. business newspaper owned by News Corp (Rupert Murdoch). Covers AI extensively through its tech and business desks and has employed prominent AI journalists including Karen Hao (China tech, 2022–2023). Has adopted AI-generated tools internally (Narrativa for market summaries, AI article summaries) and its editor-in-chief Emma Tucker has been openly pro-AI in newsrooms. Editorial coverage is business-focused and generally frames AI as an economic and competitive opportunity.

name: Leading the Future
notes:
Leading the Future (FEC ID: C00916114) is a bipartisan super PAC launched by a coalition of AI companies and industry leaders with the mission to ensure U.S. leadership in AI innovation and development. It engages in independent expenditures to support candidates aligned with pro-AI agendas, legislative advocacy, and grassroots efforts across federal and state levels, with operations beginning in 2025 and plans to expand nationwide in 2026.

Registered as a federal super PAC (FEC ID: C00916114). Classified as a pro-innovation AI policy PAC. See https://elections.transformernews.ai/ for live spending data.

name: Public First Action
notes: Bipartisan 501(c)(4) nonprofit focused on AI policy advocacy, co-founded in 2025 by former Congressmen Brad Carson (D) and Chris Stewart (R). Received $20M from Anthropic in Feb 2026 — its largest single donation — to counter 'Leading the Future,' a $125M super PAC backed by tech billionaires opposing AI regulation. Policy priorities: AI model transparency safeguards, federal AI governance framework, smart export controls on AI chips, targeted regulation of near-term high risks (bioweapons, cyberattacks). Plans to back 30-50 candidates from both parties in 2026 state and federal races.

name: Brookings Institution
notes: The Brookings Institution is an American think tank in Washington, D.C. that conducts research and education in the social sciences, primarily in economics and tax policy, metropolitan policy, governance, foreign policy, global economy, and economic development. The organization was founded in 1916 as the Institute for Government Research (IGR), with the mission of becoming "the first private organization devoted to analyzing public policy issues at the national level". Brookings states that its staff "represent diverse points of view" and describes itself as nonpartisan. The institution is currently led by President Cecilia Rouse.

name: Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET)
notes: The Center for Security and Emerging Technology (CSET) is a policy research organization within Georgetown University's Walsh School of Foreign Service that was founded in 2019. When founded, it was Washington's first think tank focused entirely on security and emerging technology, and it quickly grew to be the largest research organization in the world working on AI policy. CSET provides decision-makers with data-driven analysis on the security implications of emerging technologies, currently focusing on the effects of progress in artificial intelligence (AI), advanced computing and biotechnology. The organization has received more than $100 million in funding through 2025, with Open Philanthropy as its main funder.

name: Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS)
notes: Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS) is a bipartisan Washington, D.C.-based think tank founded in 1962. One of the leading U.S. foreign-policy and national-security research institutions. On AI, CSIS houses the Wadhwani AI Center (launched 2024, directed by Gregory C. Allen) which publishes research on AI and national security, chip export controls, and U.S.-China technology competition, and produces the biweekly CSIS AI Policy Podcast.

name: Council on Foreign Relations
notes: The Council on Foreign Relations (CFR) is a nonpartisan, independent national membership organization, think tank, educator, and publisher founded in 1921. CFR publishes Foreign Affairs magazine and generates policy-relevant ideas and analysis on the most consequential issues facing the United States and the world. The organization has 1,277 employees and an annual revenue of $77.3 million. CFR's mission is to inform U.S. engagement with the world and convenes experts and policymakers to promote informed public discussion.

name: RAND Corporation
notes: RAND Corporation is a nonprofit, nonpartisan research organization that provides leaders with the information they need to make evidence-based decisions. The organization studies the potential opportunities and risks of artificial intelligence, including ways to strengthen the AI workforce, countering AI-driven social media manipulation by U.S. adversaries, AI governance and how to make future AI systems safer, potential military applications of AI, how AI is being used in the classroom, and identifying occupations at risk of being replaced by AI. RAND operates the RAND Center on AI, Security, and Technology (RAND CAST), which is at the forefront of charting the path for a flourishing future in an era of rapid technological change. The organization was founded in 1948 as an independent nonprofit corporation, separating from the Douglas Aircraft Company.

name: Andreessen Horowitz (a16z)
notes: Andreessen Horowitz (a16z) is a private American venture capital firm founded in 2009 by Marc Andreessen and Ben Horowitz. The firm raised over $15 billion across multiple funds in 2025, representing over 18% of all venture capital dollars allocated in the United States that year. a16z has over $90B+ under management across multiple funds as of March 5, 2026. The firm focuses on investing across AI, bio + healthcare, consumer, crypto, enterprise, fintech, games, infrastructure, and companies building toward American dynamism.

name: BlackRock
notes: BlackRock is a global asset manager and technology provider with over $14 trillion in assets under management as of Q4 2025. The company has formed an AI Infrastructure Partnership with Microsoft, raising $12.5 billion toward a $30 billion goal. BlackRock has built an AI research platform dubbed 'Asimov' for use in its fundamental equity business. The company's CEO Larry Fink has framed AI as 'the most significant technology since, at least, the computer' in his 2026 annual letter to investors.

name: Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy)
notes: Coefficient Giving (formerly Open Philanthropy) is an American philanthropic advising and funding organization with CEO Alexander Berger. As of June 2025, Coefficient Giving has directed more than $4 billion in grants across various focus areas including global health, scientific research, pandemic preparedness, potential risks from advanced AI, and farm animal welfare. In November 2025, Open Philanthropy was renamed Coefficient Giving, reflecting its evolution from working primarily with one philanthropic partner to supporting multiple major donors pursuing cost-effective, high-impact giving. The organization's founding and most significant ongoing partnership is with Good Ventures, the foundation of Cari Tuna and Dustin Moskovitz.

name: Founders Fund
notes: Founders Fund is an American venture capital fund formed in 2005 and based in San Francisco, with roughly $17 billion in total assets under management as of 2025. The firm is closing in on a $6 billion fundraise for its fourth growth vehicle, with this rapid-fire pace happening less than a year after it wrapped up its previous $4.6 billion fund. Founders Fund was the first institutional investor in Space Exploration Technologies (SpaceX) and Palantir Technologies, and an early investor in Facebook. The firm's investments include Airbnb, Anduril, DeepMind, Rippling, Facebook, Ramp, Palantir Technologies, Pudgy Penguins, SpaceX, Spotify, Stripe, Neuralink, and Nubank.

name: Gates Foundation
notes: The Gates Foundation is an American private foundation founded by Bill Gates and Melinda French Gates, based in Seattle, Washington, launched in 2000 and reported to be the third-wealthiest charitable foundation in the world, holding $86 billion in assets as of July 31, 2025. The foundation's primary stated goals are to enhance healthcare and reduce extreme poverty across the world, and to expand educational opportunities and access to information technology in the United States. In May 2025, Bill Gates announced the foundation will spend $200 billion over the next 20 years and set a new date of 2045 to sunset its operations. The foundation's 2025 budget reached $8.74 billion, which is the largest budget in its history.

name: Public Investment Fund
notes: Saudi Arabia sovereign wealth fund (~$930B AUM). Launched Humain (May 2025) as full-stack AI company — data centers, cloud, models (ALLAM Arabic LLM), applications. $40B AI investment fund. Strategic partnership with NVIDIA (500MW GPU capacity over 5 years). $10B joint investment with Google Cloud for AI hub. Aramco acquired minority stake in Humain. Central to Vision 2030 economic diversification. Chaired by Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman.

name: Sequoia Capital
notes: Founded 1972, manages ~$85B in assets. Major AI investments include OpenAI, Anthropic, Reflection AI, and Ricursive Intelligence. Published influential AI market analyses including 'Generative AI's Act Two' and '2026: This is AGI' defining AGI as 'the ability to figure things out.' Portfolio companies account for 25% of NASDAQ's total value.

name: SoftBank
notes: Founded 1981, manages $140+ billion through Vision Funds. Major AI investments include $64.6 billion in OpenAI (13% ownership), $1.4 billion in Skild AI, and leadership in the $500 billion Stargate AI infrastructure project. Portfolio includes 300+ AI and tech companies across foundation models, robotics, and infrastructure.

name: Daron Acemoglu
title: Institute Professor of Economics, MIT; Nobel Prize Winner in Economic Sciences (2024)
other_orgs: MIT Stone Center on Inequality and Shaping the Future of Work (Faculty Co-Director), MIT Blueprint Labs (Research Affiliate), National Academy of Sciences (Fellow), American Philosophical Society
notes: Daron Acemoglu is an Institute Professor at MIT who won the 2024 Nobel Prize in Economics with Simon Johnson and James A. Robinson for studies of how institutions are formed and affect prosperity. He argues that AI is not improving productivity as much as hyped, projecting only modest GDP gains of 1.1% to 1.6% over the next decade. Acemoglu believes current AI development focuses too much on automation rather than complementing human workers. He co-authored the book 'Power and Progress: Our Thousand-Year Struggle Over Technology and Prosperity' with Simon Johnson.

name: Fei-Fei Li
title: Co-Director, Stanford Institute for Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence; Co-founder, World Labs
other_orgs: Former Chief Scientist at Google Cloud (2017-2018), Co-founder AI4ALL (2017), Joint California Policy Working Group on AI Frontier Models Co-Lead (2024-2025), Former Director Stanford AI Lab (201
notes: Fei-Fei Li is a Chinese-born American computer scientist best known for establishing ImageNet, the dataset that enabled rapid advances in computer vision in the 2010s. She is a professor of computer science at Stanford University, with research expertise in artificial intelligence, machine learning, deep learning, computer vision, and cognitive neuroscience. She has been called the godmother of AI for her pioneering work in computer vision and image recognition. In 2024, she raised $230 million for a startup called World Labs, which she and three colleagues founded to develop a "spatial intelligence" AI technology.

name: Geoffrey Hinton
title: Professor Emeritus, University of Toronto; Former VP & Engineering Fellow, Google; 2024 Nobel Prize Laureate in Physics
other_orgs: Vector Institute (Co-founder, Chief Scientific Advisor), Schwartz Reisman Institute for Technology and Society, Canadian Institute for Advanced Research (CIFAR), AI Safety Foundation
notes: Left Google in May 2023 to speak freely about AI risks after decades of pioneering neural network research. Estimates 10-20% chance of human extinction from superintelligent AI and 50% probability AI will surpass human intelligence within 20 years. Testified to Senate Judiciary Committee in December 2025 and receives funding from Good Ventures foundation ($700,000) to continue AI safety advocacy work.

name: Stuart Russell
title: Professor of Computer Science, UC Berkeley / Director, Center for Human-Compatible AI / President, International Association for Safe & Ethical AI
other_orgs: Distinguished Professor of Computational Precision Health at UCSF, Co-chair of World Economic Forum's Council on AI, Co-chair of OECD Expert Group on AI Futures, US representative at Global Partn
notes: Stuart Russell is a professor of computer science at the University of California, Berkeley and holds the Smith-Zadeh Chair in Engineering. He co-authored the textbook 'Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach' with Peter Norvig, which is used in over 1,500 universities in 135 countries. Russell founded the Center for Human-Compatible Artificial Intelligence at UC Berkeley in 2016 and serves as its director. He is president of the International Association for Safe and Ethical AI, which he co-founded.

name: Yoshua Bengio
title: Full Professor & Scientific Director of Mila, Chair of International AI Safety Report, Co-President & Scientific Director of LawZero
other_orgs: Canada CIFAR AI Chair, Senior Fellow at CIFAR, Special Advisor and Founding Scientific Director of IVADO, Member of UN Scientific Advisory Board for Independent Advice on Breakthroughs in Science
notes: Yoshua Bengio is a Canadian computer scientist born March 5, 1964, in Paris, France, and is a full professor at Université de Montréal. He received the 2018 A.M. Turing Award together with Geoffrey Hinton and Yann LeCun for their foundational work on deep learning. In June 2025, Bengio launched a nonprofit organization called LawZero aimed at building 'honest' AI systems that can detect and block harmful behavior by autonomous agents. He chairs the International AI Safety Report, an annual study that seeks to compile scientific evidence on emerging AI risks.

name: Yuval Noah Harari
title: Historian & Author; Co-founder of Sapienship; Distinguished Research Fellow at Cambridge Centre for the Study of Existential Risk
other_orgs: Hebrew University of Jerusalem (Lecturer), University of Cambridge Centre for the Study of Existential Risk (Distinguished Research Fellow), Sapienship (Co-founder)
notes: Delivered major AI warnings at Davos 2026 WEF, predicting AI will create identity and immigration crises for every country. Warns that 'anything made of words will be taken over by AI' including laws, books, and religions. Has sold over 50 million books in 65 languages and regularly advises world leaders including Emmanuel Macron and Angela Merkel. Published 'Nexus: A Brief History of Information Networks from the Stone Age to AI' in 2024.

name: Andy Jassy
title: President and CEO, Amazon
other_orgs: Former member of National Security Commission on Artificial Intelligence (2018-2021), Member of The Business Council, Chairman of Rainier Prep charter school
notes: Leads Amazon's massive AI investments including $100+ billion in capital expenditures for 2025, predominantly for AWS AI capabilities. Has developed Amazon's Frontier Model Safety Framework with specific critical capability thresholds and safeguards. Joined US AI Safety Institute Consortium in 2024 and contributed $5 million in compute credits for AI safety evaluation tools. Emphasized urgent need for companies to invest 'aggressively' in AI infrastructure while maintaining responsible development practices.

name: Dario Amodei
title: CEO and Co-founder
other_orgs: Former VP Research at OpenAI (2016-2020), Former Senior Research Scientist at Google Brain (2015-2016), Former researcher at Baidu (2014-2015), Postdoctoral scholar at Stanford University School
notes: Dario Amodei (born 1983) is an American artificial intelligence researcher and entrepreneur who co-founded Anthropic in 2021 with his sister Daniela Amodei. He previously served as vice president of research at OpenAI, where he led the development of large language models like GPT-2 and GPT-3. Amodei earned his doctorate degree in biophysics from Princeton University as a Hertz Fellow, and was a postdoctoral scholar at the Stanford University School of Medicine. In 2025, Time magazine listed Amodei as one of the world's 100 most influential people.

name: Demis Hassabis
title: CEO and Co-Founder, Google DeepMind
other_orgs: Co-founder and CEO of Isomorphic Labs, UK Government AI Adviser, Fellow of the Royal Society, former visiting scientist at MIT and Harvard
notes: Demis Hassabis is the CEO and co-founder of Google DeepMind, an artificial intelligence company that was founded in London in 2010 and acquired by Google in 2014. He shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry with John M. Jumper for their work on AlphaFold, an AI tool that predicts protein structures. He is also the founder and CEO of Isomorphic Labs, a biotechnology company focused on drug discovery. Hassabis was born on July 27, 1976, in London to a Greek Cypriot father and Chinese Singaporean mother.

name: Elon Musk
title: Founder and CEO, xAI; CEO and Product Architect, Tesla; Founder, CEO, and Chief Engineer, SpaceX
other_orgs: X Corp. (owner), Neuralink (co-founder), The Boring Company (founder), OpenAI (co-founder, departed 2018), Tesla (CEO and Product Architect), SpaceX (founder and CEO), Former Senior Advisor to Pr
notes: Elon Musk is a businessman and entrepreneur known for his leadership of Tesla, SpaceX, X, and xAI. Musk has been the wealthiest person in the world since 2025; as of April 2026, Forbes estimates his net worth to be US$809 billion. He made predictions at the World Economic Forum that artificial intelligence will be smarter than all of humanity by the end of the decade. Musk spent more than $290 million on the 2024 election, boosting President Donald Trump and Republicans.

name: Jensen Huang
title: Founder, President & CEO, Nvidia
other_orgs: National Academy of Engineering (member), Various AI industry partnerships including Partnership on AI
notes: In January 2026 stated 'I think we've achieved AGI' on Lex Fridman podcast. Predicted AGI by 2028 at November 2023 NYT DealBook Summit. Advocates against 'doomer narratives' about AI, arguing excessive fear-mongering deters investment and slows safety innovation. Met with President Trump in December 2025 to discuss chip export controls and AI policy. Committed to building $500 billion in AI supercomputers during Trump's term to reindustrialize America.

name: Joe Biden
notes: 46th U.S. President (2021-2025). Issued Executive Order 14110 in October 2023, the most comprehensive U.S. AI governance action to that point, requiring safety reporting from frontier AI developers and coordinating federal agency standards. Also released the Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights in 2022.

name: Mark Zuckerberg
title: CEO and Chairman, Meta Platforms
other_orgs: Chan Zuckerberg Initiative (Co-founder and Co-CEO), Fwd.US (Founder), Breakthrough Starshot (Board Member)
notes: Mark Zuckerberg is building an artificial intelligence agent to help him perform his executive duties autonomously, according to multiple reports. The AI agent helps Zuckerberg get information faster, such as by retrieving answers for him that he would typically have to go through layers of people to get. Zuckerberg serves as CEO of both Facebook and its parent company Meta Platforms. He is married to Priscilla Chan and they have three daughters.

name: Sam Altman
title: CEO of OpenAI
other_orgs: Chairman of Helion Energy, Co-founder of Worldcoin, Former President of Y Combinator (2014-2019), Co-founder of Hydrazine Capital, Board member at Reddit (until 2021), Former board member at Oklo
notes: Sam Altman is the CEO of OpenAI, the artificial intelligence research organization that developed ChatGPT. He became CEO of OpenAI in 2019 after co-founding the organization in 2015. In November 2023, he was briefly fired by OpenAI's board of directors but was reinstated five days later. His net worth was estimated at US$3.3 billion by Forbes in March 2026.

name: Satya Nadella
title: Chairman and CEO, Microsoft
other_orgs: Former Board of Directors at Starbucks (2017-2024), Board of Trustees at Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center, Board of Trustees at University of Chicago, Former Chairman of The Business Council (2021-2
notes: Led Microsoft's $13B+ investment in OpenAI partnership since 2019. Established joint Microsoft-OpenAI Deployment Safety Board in 2021 for pre-release safety reviews. Published 'Governing AI: A Blueprint for the Future' (2023) outlining five-point regulatory framework. Under his leadership, Microsoft has nearly 350 employees working on responsible AI. Made voluntary commitments at 2023 White House AI Safety Summit including annual transparency reporting and pre-deployment safety testing.

name: Sundar Pichai
title: CEO of Google and Alphabet Inc.
other_orgs: Former McKinsey & Company consultant, Former Applied Materials engineer
notes: CEO of Google since 2015 and Alphabet since 2019. Born in Chennai, India, earned bachelor's in metallurgy from IIT Kharagpur (1993), master's from Stanford (1995), MBA from Wharton (2002). Joined Google in 2004, led development of Chrome browser and Android. Announced $15 billion investment in India AI infrastructure including hub in Visakhapatnam. Became billionaire in 2025, earned $226 million in 2022. Led Google through major AI race with launch of Bard/Gemini chatbot.

name: Bill Gates
title: Co-chair, Gates Foundation; Co-founder, Microsoft; Founder, Breakthrough Energy
other_orgs: Breakthrough Energy, Gates Ventures, Berkshire Hathaway (board member 2004-present), Cascade Investment, Giving Pledge co-founder
notes: Gates Foundation portfolio exceeds $41.8 billion with 25.55% allocated to Microsoft, reflecting concentrated bet on AI infrastructure. Led $11.8 billion OpenAI investment through Microsoft. Portfolio includes Berkshire Hathaway (26%), Waste Management (19%), and Canadian National Railway (15%). In Q3 2025, sold $8.67 billion in Microsoft shares for portfolio rebalancing. Co-founded Giving Pledge in 2010 with Warren Buffett. Awarded Presidential Medal of Freedom (2016), Padma Bhushan (2015), and Honorary Knight Commander of the British Empire (2005).

name: Marc Andreessen
title: Co-Founder & General Partner, Andreessen Horowitz (a16z)
other_orgs: Former Co-Founder of Netscape, Former Co-Founder/CEO of Opsware (sold to HP for $1.6B), Former Co-Founder of Ning, Board member of eBay (2008-2014), Board member of Meta/Facebook, Advisor to Trum
notes: Marc Andreessen is co-founder of Netscape and co-founder of the venture capital firm Andreessen Horowitz (a16z). He is described as a founder, investor, and co-founder of Netscape, as well as co-founder of the venture capital firm Andreessen Horowitz (a16z). According to Wikipedia, he is an American businessman, venture capitalist, and former software engineer who is the co-author of Mosaic, the first web browser to display inline images. His net worth is estimated at $1.9 billion as of January 2025.

name: Masayoshi Son
title: Founder, Chairman, and CEO, SoftBank Group Corp.; Chairman, Arm Holdings; Chairman, Stargate LLC
other_orgs: Arm Holdings, Stargate LLC, SB OpenAI Japan
notes: Son has committed over $40 billion to OpenAI and leads the $500 billion Stargate AI infrastructure project with Oracle and OpenAI. He predicts AGI will arrive 'much sooner' than 2-3 years and ASI will be '10,000 times smarter' than humans by 2035. SoftBank established comprehensive AI ethics policies in 2022 applying to 88 group companies and created an AI Ethics Committee in 2024.

name: Peter Thiel
title: Co-founder and Chairman, Palantir Technologies; Managing Partner, Founders Fund
other_orgs: Palantir Technologies (Chairman), Thiel Capital, Valar Ventures, Mithril Capital, former Facebook board member (2005-2022), former Y Combinator partner (2015-2017)
notes: Peter Thiel is the billionaire co-founder of PayPal and chairman of Palantir. He argues that artificial intelligence is targeting 'math people' - coders, engineers, quants, data scientists, and algorithmic specialists - rather than creative writers, artists, or storytellers first. In a 2019 talk, Thiel suggested AI itself might be inherently authoritarian, calling AI 'communist' and warning it could bring back a world of 'a few large companies, a few large governments, a few large computers that controlled everything'. He has been giving lectures on the Antichrist, first in San Francisco in September 2025, then in Paris in January, and most recently in Rome, where his Antichrist is described as 'a Luddite who wants to stop all science'.

name: Ezra Klein
title: NYT columnist and podcast host
other_orgs: Co-founder of Vox Media (2014), former Washington Post Wonkblog editor, former American Prospect associate editor, MSNBC contributor, Bloomberg News contributor, executive producer of Netflix's '
notes: Ezra Klein is a New York Times columnist, best-selling author, and host of The Ezra Klein Show podcast. He is a co-founder of Vox and was formerly the website's editor-at-large. He has written two books: Why We're Polarized in January 2020, and Abundance, cowritten with Derek Thompson, in March 2025. Klein is married to Annie Lowrey, who writes about economics at The Atlantic, and they have two children.

name: Kara Swisher
title: Host, On with Kara Swisher & Pivot Podcasts; Technology Journalist and Author
other_orgs: New York Magazine, Former co-founder of Recode
notes: In February 2024 interviews, called for government dialogue with tech companies about AI regulation, comparing it to regulation in other industries. Argued that AI creators should not receive blanket free speech protections and advocated for legal liability for harmful AI-generated content like deepfakes. Has been covering tech industry since 1990s and regularly interviews major AI company CEOs including Sam Altman.

name: Fran Drescher
title: President of SAG-AFTRA
other_orgs: Former actress and creator of 'The Nanny' sitcom, cancer awareness advocate
notes: Fran Drescher is the SAG-AFTRA President who led the union during a 118-day strike and warned about AI threats to the entertainment industry. She became one of the prominent faces of the strike alongside WGA West President Meredith Stiehm and WGA East President Lisa Takeuchi Cullen. Drescher took office as president on October 15, 2021, and led the union during the five-month actors' strike that began on July 14, 2023. She has been vocal about protecting actors from AI technology and securing proper compensation and consent for use of their likeness.

name: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez
title: U.S. Representative, NY-14
other_orgs: Democratic Socialists of America (member), Justice Democrats (endorsed by), Sunrise Movement (supported by)
notes: Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) announced the Artificial Intelligence (AI) Data Center Moratorium Act on March 25, 2026, alongside Sen. Bernie Sanders. She announced on Friday that she is jumping into the race to be the top Democrat on the influential House Oversight Committee. Ocasio-Cortez was born on October 13, 1989, in the Bronx, New York, and began representing New York's 14th congressional district in 2019. Taking office at age 29, she became the youngest woman ever elected to Congress and is a member of the Democratic Party.

name: Bernie Sanders
title: U.S. Senator from Vermont, Independent (Senior Senator)
other_orgs: Congressional Progressive Caucus (co-founder), Senate Health, Education, Labor and Pensions Committee (former Chair 2023-2025), Senate Budget Committee (former Chair 2021-2023), Senate Veterans'
notes: Bernie Sanders is an American politician serving as the senior United States senator from Vermont, a seat he has held since 2007. He is the longest-serving independent in U.S. congressional history. Sanders was reelected to a fourth Senate term in 2024, defeating Republican Gerald Malloy. He currently serves as the Ranking Member of the Senate Health, Education, Labor and Pensions Committee after previously chairing it from 2023 to 2025.

name: Chuck Schumer
title: U.S. Senator, NY; Senate Majority Leader (2021-2025), Senate Minority Leader (2017-2021, 2025-present)
other_orgs: Bipartisan Senate AI Working Group (Co-founder/Leader), Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee (Former Chair 2005-2009), Senate Finance Committee, Senate Rules Committee
notes: Chuck Schumer is a Democratic U.S. Senator from New York who has served since 1999 and is currently Senate Majority Leader. He led a bipartisan working group that released a 31-page AI policy roadmap in May 2024, recommending at least $32 billion in government spending to accelerate AI research and development. The roadmap was developed after months of AI Insight Forums with tech companies, civil rights leaders, and other stakeholders. Schumer has described regulating artificial intelligence as "a challenge for Congress unlike any other" and has made election protection from AI interference a high priority.

name: Donald Trump
notes: Donald Trump served as the 45th President of the United States from 2017 to 2021 and was inaugurated as the 47th president on January 20, 2025. On March 20, 2026, the Trump Administration released a National AI Legislative Framework that addresses six key objectives including protecting children, safeguarding American communities, and ensuring American AI dominance. In December 2025, President Trump signed an Executive Order to protect American AI innovation from inconsistent state laws and directed the Attorney General to establish an AI Litigation Task Force. Trump has appointed David Sacks as White House AI 'Czar' who has called the administration's AI policy the '5 Cs': child safety, communities, creators, censorship, and competitiveness.

name: Elizabeth Warren
notes: Democratic U.S. Senator from Massachusetts (2013-present). Major voice on AI antitrust and competition policy. Co-introduced Protecting AI and Cloud Competition in Defense Act with Sen. Schmitt (Dec 2024, reintroduced May 2025) preventing big tech monopolies in DoD AI procurement. Co-sponsored GAIN AI Act ensuring U.S. customers get priority access to advanced AI chips over adversaries. Launched investigation (2024) into Google-Anthropic and Microsoft-OpenAI partnerships with Sen. Wyden. Wrote DOJ supporting Nvidia antitrust probe (Sep 2024). Pressed OpenAI on whistleblower protections with Rep. Trahan (Aug 2024). Co-sponsor of AI Civil Rights Act (Dec 2025). Led opposition (with Sen. Markey, Nov 2025) to big tech's attempt to insert 10-year AI regulation moratorium into NDAA.

name: Gavin Newsom
title: Governor of California
other_orgs: California Innovation Council, PlumpJack Group (founder), Getty family business interests
notes: Gavin Newsom is the 40th Governor of California who took office in 2019 and is currently serving his second term. He signed executive order N-5-26 on March 30, 2026 to regulate artificial intelligence implications prior to California government contracts, emphasizing public safety and misuse. California is home to 33 of the top 50 privately held AI companies around the globe and leads with 25% of all AI patents, conference papers, and companies. He announced California has signed agreements with four of the largest technology companies in the GenAI sector including Google, Adobe, IBM, and Microsoft to help train California's workforce for AI jobs.

name: John Thune
notes: Senate Majority Leader (Jan 2025-present), senior U.S. Senator from South Dakota. Key AI legislation gatekeeper — co-authoring bipartisan 'light-touch' AI bill with Sen. Klobuchar requiring risk/impact assessments and self-certification for critical-impact AI systems. Opposed 10-year moratorium on state AI regulation authority. Supported GAIN AI Act in FY2026 NDAA to keep advanced AI chips available to U.S. innovators over adversaries. Previously chaired Senate Commerce Committee where he led early AI policy work.

name: Marco Rubio
title: U.S. Secretary of State (2025-present), Acting National Security Advisor (February-August 2025), Former U.S. Senator from Florida (2011-2025)
other_orgs: Florida International University (adjunct professor), Vice Chair of Senate Intelligence Committee (2021-2025), Senate Foreign Relations Committee, Senate Small Business and Entrepreneurship Commi
notes: First Latino to serve as U.S. Secretary of State, unanimously confirmed January 25, 2025. Served as Vice Chair of Senate Intelligence Committee (2021-2025) with focus on AI national security and U.S.-China competition. Described as 'virtual secretary of state for Latin America' during Trump's first term. Banned from entering China due to sanctions imposed twice in 2020. JD degree from University of Miami (1996), BA from University of Florida (1993).

name: Mike Johnson
notes: Speaker of the U.S. House of Representatives (Republican, Louisiana). Leading congressional effort to implement the Trump administration national AI framework. Outlined a 3-point plan for AI: (1) treat AI as a matter of national security, (2) keep AI in U.S. and allied hands, (3) move "at the speed that victory demands." Stated "America will win the AI race if government resists the siren song of control, and if industry steps up as our patriotic partner." Praised the White House AI roadmap for providing "innovators with much-needed certainty, while protecting consumers and prioritizing kids online safety." Supports federal preemption of state AI regulations.

name: Ted Cruz
title: U.S. Senator from Texas, Chairman of Senate Commerce Committee
other_orgs: Former Texas Solicitor General (2003-2008), Former George W. Bush administration advisor, Former Federal Trade Commission and Department of Justice attorney
notes: Ted Cruz serves as U.S. Senate Commerce Committee Chairman and released a legislative framework designed to promote American leadership in artificial intelligence on September 10, 2025. He introduced the SANDBOX Act, which creates a regulatory "sandbox" that gives AI developers space to test and launch new AI technologies without being held back by outdated or inflexible federal rules. Cruz is weighing a second presidential bid according to sources close to the senator, which would put him on course to collide with Vice President JD Vance. He has positioned himself as a vocal champion of traditional Republican foreign policy positions and has criticized Tucker Carlson, calling him "a coward" during a speech before a group supporting Jewish causes.

name: Andrej Karpathy
title: AI Researcher, Educator & Founder of Eureka Labs
other_orgs: Former OpenAI founding member (2015-2017), Former Tesla AI Director (2017-2022), Stanford University (CS231n instructor), Former Google Brain & DeepMind intern
notes: Founded Eureka Labs (2024) focused on AI-native education after leaving Tesla where he directed Autopilot AI development (2017-2022). PhD from Stanford (2016) under Fei-Fei Li, designed influential CS231n deep learning course. Founding member of OpenAI (2015-2017). Created viral 'Neural Networks: Zero to Hero' YouTube series and educational content reaching millions. Has 500K+ Twitter followers and is considered one of the most influential AI educators globally.

name: Ilya Sutskever
title: Co-founder & CEO, Safe Superintelligence Inc; Former Chief Scientist & Co-founder, OpenAI
other_orgs: Former Google Brain researcher, University of Toronto (PhD under Geoffrey Hinton)
notes: Co-invented AlexNet (2012) and made foundational contributions to transformer architecture and GPT models. Participated in November 2023 board vote to temporarily oust Sam Altman from OpenAI over safety concerns. Founded SSI in June 2024 with $1 billion initial funding, reaching $30+ billion valuation by 2025. Claims 'age of scaling is over' and that new research breakthroughs beyond current paradigms are needed for safe superintelligence.

name: Jeff Dean
notes: Chief Scientist at Google DeepMind (appointed 2023 after merger of DeepMind and Google Brain). Co-founded Google Brain in 2011. Named to TIME100 AI 2025 list. One of the most influential figures in modern AI — key architect of MapReduce, TensorFlow, and transformer-era Google AI. Active angel investor in AI startups. On AI safety, takes a balanced middle-ground position: recognizes risks from misinformation and automated hacking, advocates for safeguards and mitigations, but rejects extreme precautionary approaches. Supports Google's Responsible AI principles framework. Signed amicus brief supporting Anthropic. Believes AI can accelerate scientific discovery 10-100x for climate, disease, energy.

name: John M. Jumper
notes: Director at Google DeepMind. Led development of AlphaFold (protein structure prediction). Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2024 (shared with Demis Hassabis and David Baker). PhD theoretical chemistry (U of Chicago). AI optimist — believes AI has "unparalleled potential to improve the lives of billions."

name: Yann LeCun
title: Chief AI Scientist (former), Meta AI; Silver Professor, NYU; Founder and Executive Chairman, Advanced Machine Intelligence Labs (AMI Labs)
other_orgs: New York University Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences, NYU Center for Data Science (founding director 2012-2014), Advanced Machine Intelligence Labs (AMI Labs, founder/executive chairman
notes: Yann LeCun is Chief AI Scientist at Meta and Silver Professor at New York University affiliated with the NYU Center for Data Science and the Courant Institute of Mathematical Sciences. He received the 2018 ACM Turing Award shared with Geoffrey Hinton and Yoshua Bengio for contributions to deep learning. He announced plans to leave Meta after ten years to found his own company focused on world-model architectures called Advanced Machine Intelligence Labs (AMI Labs). AMI Labs raised $1.03 billion in funding at a $3.5 billion pre-money valuation in March 2026.

name: Attention Is All You Need
resource_author: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Łukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin (Google)
notes: 2017 Google paper at NeurIPS that introduced the Transformer architecture — the foundation of GPT, BERT, Claude, Gemini, LLaMA, and essentially every frontier language model. Of the eight authors, Aidan N. Gomez later co-founded Cohere. The paper is routinely invoked in AI policy discussions to ground claims about the lineage and maturity of current AI systems.

name: EU AI Act
notes: The European Union''s comprehensive AI regulation framework, entered into force in August 2024, establishing a risk-based classification system for AI applications with tiered requirements ranging from transparency obligations to outright bans on certain uses.

name: Executive Order on Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy AI
resource_title: Executive Order on Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy AI
notes: The formal title of President Biden''s October 2023 Executive Order (EO 14110) on artificial intelligence, establishing federal standards for AI safety, security, and trustworthiness.

name: Human Compatible
notes: TEST DATA — Foundational text on AI alignment for general audience

name: International AI Safety Report
notes: A 2024 international report led by Yoshua Bengio and a global panel of experts assessing the current state of AI safety risks and recommending governance approaches.

name: NIST AI Risk Management Framework
notes: TEST DATA — De facto standard for AI risk management in US industry

name: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
resource_title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
resource_author: Jared Kaplan, Sam McCandlish, Tom Henighan, Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Chess, Rewon Child, Scott Gray, Alec Radford, Jeffrey Wu, Dario Amodei (OpenAI)
notes: 2020 OpenAI paper by Jared Kaplan, Sam McCandlish, and team (many of whom later co-founded Anthropic) that established the empirical scaling-laws framework. A foundational reference for policy debates about compute governance, export controls, and the inevitability/contingency of capability gains. Widely cited alongside the Chinchilla paper (Hoffmann et al. 2022).

name: Statement on AI Risk
notes: TEST DATA — One-sentence statement signed by hundreds of AI researchers and public figures

name: Superintelligence: Paths, Dangers, Strategies
notes: A 2014 book by Oxford philosopher Nick Bostrom analyzing the potential trajectories, dangers, and strategic challenges posed by the development of machine superintelligence.

name: Trump AI Action Plan (Jan 2025)
notes: Executive order signed by President Trump in January 2025 establishing the administration''s AI policy direction, emphasizing deregulation and American competitiveness in AI development.
